rationale: The citing paper uses Grewal, Mehta, and Kardes (2004) in two meaningful ways: as empirical support that attitude functions can predict consequential consumer behaviors, and as a direct source for adapted value-expressive and social-adjustive attitude-function measures in Study 1. This goes beyond a background name-check because the cited paper helps justify the paper's hypothesis logic and operationalization of core constructs. The 2009 study then transfers these attitude-function constructs to a new domain, counterfeit luxury consumption.
relation_description: The citing paper applies the attitude-function logic and measurement approach from Grewal et al. (2004) to examine counterfeit luxury purchase intentions and preference changes for genuine brands. It uses the cited work as prior evidence and as a measurement template in building its own tests.
